name: Deianira
description: Deianira, meaning "destroyer of her husband," was a princess of Calydon in Greek mythology and the wife of Heracles. She was the daughter of King Oeneus of Calydon and Althaea, though it was rumored that the god Dionysus was her true father. Deianira was known for her beauty and skill at driving a chariot. Her suitors included Achelous, a river god, whom Heracles famously wrestled for her hand, winning her as his wife. Their marriage was initially happy, but it was plagued by Heracles's numerous labors and infidelities. While traveling, the centaur Nessus attempted to assault Deianira as he ferried her across a river. Heracles shot Nessus with a poisoned arrow, and as he lay dying, Nessus tricked Deianira into collecting his blood, claiming it was a love potion that would ensure Heracles's fidelity. Years later, when Heracles seemed to favor Iole, Deianira, fearing she had lost his love, smeared a robe with Nessus's blood, hoping to rekindle his affection. However, the blood was a potent poison, and the robe caused Heracles excruciating pain, ultimately leading to his death. Overwhelmed by guilt and remorse, Deianira took her own life. Her tragic story serves as a cautionary tale about jealousy, deception, and the unintended consequences of well-intentioned actions.
subtitle: Tragic wife of Heracles.

keyMyths.title: The Contest for Deianira
keyMyths.description: Deianira was pursued by both Achelous, a river god, and Heracles. Heracles wrestled Achelous, defeating him and winning Deianira's hand in marriage, demonstrating his strength and heroism.

keyMyths.title: The Centaur Nessus
keyMyths.description: While crossing a river, the centaur Nessus attempted to assault Deianira. Heracles shot Nessus with a poisoned arrow, but the dying centaur tricked Deianira into collecting his blood, claiming it was a love potion.

keyMyths.title: Deianira's Fatal Mistake
keyMyths.description: Fearing she had lost Heracles's love to Iole, Deianira used Nessus's blood on a robe for Heracles. The robe poisoned Heracles, causing him immense suffering and ultimately leading to his death, followed by Deianira's suicide.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology.
sources.text: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: A Greek tragedy focusing on the final days of Heracles and the role of Deianira.
